name: Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi
shortDescription: A legendary sword from Japanese mythology, later known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, it is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan.
description: Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi, meaning "Sword of the Gathering Clouds of Heaven," is a legendary sword in Japanese mythology. Initially known as Amano-Murakumo, it gained the name Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi ("Grass-Cutting Sword") after Yamato Takeru used it to cut through a field of burning grass during an ambush. The sword is one of the Three Sacred Treasures (Imperial Regalia) of Japan, symbolizing valor. Its acquisition is detailed in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, where Susanoo-no-Mikoto obtains it after slaying the Yamata no Orochi. It was later presented to Amaterasu. The sword's possession signifies imperial authority, and it is traditionally housed at Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, though it is said to be rarely, if ever, displayed.
subtitle: The Cloud-Gathering Sword of Imperial Valor
characteristics: Associated with Susanoo-no-Mikoto and Yamato Takeru
significance: Symbolizes the emperor's authority and valor; crucial artifact in Japanese imperial history.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi's origin is deeply entwined with the myth of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, the storm god. After being banished from the heavens, Susanoo encountered an elderly couple weeping over the impending sacrifice of their daughter to the monstrous Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. Susanoo offered to slay the beast in exchange for their daughter's hand in marriage. He intoxicated the serpent with sake and, once it was incapacitated, used his Totsuka-no-Tsurugi to slice it into pieces. Inside one of the serpent's tails, he discovered a magnificent sword, which he presented to his sister, Amaterasu, the sun goddess, as a reconciliation offering. This sword was initially known as Amano-Murakumo. Later, it came into the possession of Yamato Takeru, a legendary prince, who used it to cut through a field of burning grass set ablaze by his enemies, thus earning it the name Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: As one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan (the others being the Yata no Kagami mirror and the Yasakani no Magatama jewel), the Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi holds immense cultural and political significance. These treasures are said to have been bestowed upon the imperial family by the gods, legitimizing their rule and symbolizing their divine lineage. The sword, in particular, represents valor and strength, qualities expected of the emperor. The sword's presence signifies the emperor's authority and right to rule. While the Yata no Kagami is enshrined at Ise Grand Shrine and the Yasakani no Magatama is located at the Imperial Palace in Tokyo, the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is traditionally housed at Atsuta Shrine. Due to its sacred nature, it is rarely, if ever, publicly displayed, adding to its mystique and reverence.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi is rich in symbolic meaning. As a sword, it inherently represents power, strength, and the ability to overcome obstacles. Its association with Susanoo-no-Mikoto, a god of storms and chaos, suggests a connection to untamed power and the ability to conquer adversity. The transformation of the sword's name to Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, the "Grass-Cutting Sword," further enhances its symbolic weight. This transformation symbolizes the ability to overcome adversity through strategic thinking and decisive action, as demonstrated by Yamato Takeru's clever use of the sword to escape the burning field. The sword embodies the virtues of courage, resilience, and the capacity to transform danger into opportunity. It also symbolizes the taming of chaotic forces into a tool for good.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi continues to exert a strong influence on Japanese culture and identity. It remains a potent symbol of imperial authority and national pride. References to the sword appear in various forms of Japanese media, including literature, anime, manga, and video games, often serving as a symbol of power or a MacGuffin driving the plot. The legend of Yamato Takeru and his use of the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is still taught in schools, reinforcing the values of courage, ingenuity, and loyalty. The Atsuta Shrine, where the sword is purportedly kept, remains a significant religious site, attracting pilgrims and tourists alike. Despite its shrouded history and the mystery surrounding its current state, the Amano-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi persists as a powerful and enduring symbol of Japan's rich mythological heritage.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi (translator)
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest extant chronicle, providing an account of the creation of Japan and the deeds of the gods and early emperors.

sources.author: W.G. Aston (translator)
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki (Nihongi), another crucial historical text with similar content to the Kojiki, offering a slightly different perspective.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Represents the burning field Yamato Takeru escaped, solidifying the name Kusanagi.

associations.name: Serpent (Yamata no Orochi)
associations.significance: The sword originated from the slain serpent, linking it to overcoming monstrous chaos.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Excalibur
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A legendary sword symbolizing royal authority and magical power.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Tyrfing
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A cursed sword with immense power and significance, similar to the power attributed to Kusanagi.

companions.name: Susanoo-no-Mikoto
companions.description: The storm god who discovered the sword within the Yamata no Orochi.

companions.name: Yamato Takeru
companions.description: The legendary prince who used the sword to cut through the burning field and renamed it Kusanagi.

cultural.modernLegacy: The sword is a recurring motif in Japanese popular culture, symbolizing power and national identity. It is often featured in anime, manga, and video games. Atsuta Shrine, where the sword is purportedly kept, is a popular pilgrimage destination.

variants: Sword of the Gathering Clouds of Heaven
